summary: Dan Luu curates a comprehensive list of programming blogs worth reading, organized by author with brief descriptions of their content focus and writing style. Rather than prescribing a universal reading list, he acknowledges that different programmers have different learning styles and interests. The post emphasizes the decline of blog discovery through blogrolls and calls for readers to share recommendations, noting that even popular blogs can fly under the radar.
key_points: Curated list of 50+ technical blogs spanning systems programming, distributed systems, performance, compilers, and tech culture
Each recommendation includes the blog's focus area, writing style, and what readers should expect in terms of difficulty
Highlights the decline of blog discovery mechanisms like blogrolls and blogging as dialogue
Openly solicits recommendations, noting that even well-known blogs can be unknown to him
money_quotes: This is one of those 'N technical things every programmer must read' lists, except that 'programmer' is way too broad a term and the styles of writing people find helpful for them are too different for any such list to contain a non-zero number of items.
He has a lot of ideas that sound like common sense, e.g., choose boring technology, until you realize that it's actually uncommon to find opinions that are so sensible.

Even with centralized link aggregators like HN and reddit, blog discovery has become haphazard and random with the decline of blogrolls and blogging as a dialogue, as opposed to the current practice of blogging as a monologue.

90% of the time, when I get the desire to write a post about a common misconception software folks have about hardware, Yossi has already written the post and taken a lot of flak for it so I don't have to.
key_insight: The fragmentation of the programming blogosphere means valuable technical writing is increasingly hard to discover, and curated lists with context about writing style and difficulty level help readers find content that matches their learning preferences.
